title: Give Useful Feedback
summary: Share constructive feedback that is specific and helpful.
learnerPromise: You will be able to give feedback that helps someone improve without making them feel attacked.
whyItMatters: Good feedback helps people grow. Vague criticism or harsh comments shut people down. Useful feedback builds trust and makes teams, friendships, and work relationships stronger.
realLifeUses: Telling a classmate what worked and what did not in a group presentation
Giving a coworker notes on a draft report
Helping a friend improve a skill they are practicing
Telling a service provider what you liked and what could be better
result: A piece of specific, constructive feedback delivered to someone
youWillLearn: Point out something specific rather than giving general criticism
Use the format 'What I noticed' and 'One idea you might try'
Balance feedback by mentioning what is working too
Deliver feedback in private and at a good time
miniChallenge: Think of someone you interact with regularly. Write one sentence of positive feedback and one sentence of constructive feedback about something specific they did.
steps: Pick a specific action or behavior to talk about, not the whole person
Start by noting something that went well or what you appreciated
Share the specific thing you noticed that could improve
Offer one practical idea or suggestion they could try
End with encouragement and offer to discuss it more if they want
completionCriteria: Your feedback refers to a specific action, not a general trait
It includes at least one positive point and one constructive suggestion
The other person receives it without feeling personally attacked
commonProblems: Giving vague feedback like 'It was fine' or 'It was bad' with no detail
Only pointing out problems and forgetting to mention what went well
Giving feedback in front of others, which can feel embarrassing
tips: The phrase 'I noticed that' keeps feedback focused on behavior, not personality
One specific suggestion is more useful than a list of everything wrong
Ask permission first if the feedback is more than casual, like 'Can I share one thought?'
